description_plain: Your child can explain patterns in zeros and decimal points when multiplying or dividing by powers of 10, using exponents to show powers of 10.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that, in a multi-digit number, a digit in a given place represents 10 times as much as it represents in the place to its right and 1/10 of what it represents in the place to its left.
description_plain: Your child understands that in a multi-digit number, a digit is 10 times bigger than the digit to its right and one-tenth the size of the digit to its left.

subject: Math
description: Understand the base ten place value system.
description_plain: Your child understands how the base ten place value system works.

subject: Math
description: Interpret a box plot to answer statistical questions about a data set.
description_plain: Your child can read a box plot and answer questions about the data it shows.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the relative frequency of an interval of data values when given a histogram.
description_plain: Your child can find the relative frequency of data intervals when looking at a histogram.

subject: Math
description: Represent numerical data on a number line, histogram, and box plot.
description_plain: Your child can show numerical data on a number line, histogram, and box plot.

subject: Math
description: Summarize and describe distributions.
description_plain: Your child can summarize and describe how data is spread out.

subject: Math
description: Determine which measure of variation (range or interquartile range) is more appropriate to describe the shape; justify the choice.
description_plain: Your child can decide whether to use range or interquartile range to describe data and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Calculate and interpret the measure of variation [range and interquartile range (IQR)] of a numerical data set.
description_plain: Your child can calculate and understand the range and interquartile range of a set of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explore range and interquartile range.
description_plain: Your child explores how to find and use range and interquartile range.

subject: Math
description: Describe how the mean or median is affected by outliers of a numerical data set.
description_plain: Your child can explain how outliers affect the mean or median of a data set.

subject: Math
description: Determine which measure of center (mean or median) is more appropriate to describe the center of data and justify the choice.
description_plain: Your child can choose whether the mean or median better describes the center of data and explain why.

subject: Math
description: Calculate and interpret any measure of center (mean, median, and mode) of a numerical data set.
description_plain: Your child can find and understand the mean, median, and mode of a set of numbers.

subject: Math
description: Explore mean, median, and mode.
description_plain: Your child explores the concepts of mean, median, and mode.

subject: Math
description: Identify the difference between statistical and non-statistical questions and write simple statistical questions that allow variable responses.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between statistical and non-statistical questions and write simple statistical questions that have different possible answers.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that data collected to answer a statistical question can be analyzed by their distributions.
description_plain: Your child understands that data collected to answer a statistical question can be studied by looking at how the data is spread out.

subject: Math
description: Convert measurements within and between the metric and customary measurement systems to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can change measurements within and between metric and customary systems to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply measurement knowledge to solve real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child uses what they know about measurement to solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Use coordinates to calculate vertical and horizontal distances between points with the same x-coordinate or the same y-coordinate to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use coordinates to find vertical and horizontal distances between points that share the same x or y value to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Draw polygons in the coordinate plane when given coordinates for the vertices.
description_plain: Your child can draw shapes on a coordinate plane when given the points for the corners.

subject: Math
description: Find and graph pairs of rational numbers in all four quadrants of the coordinate plane in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find and plot pairs of numbers on all four parts of the coordinate plane to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Graph points in all four quadrants.
description_plain: Your child can plot points on all four parts (quadrants) of the coordinate plane.

subject: Math
description: Construct nets of a rectangular prism, rectangular pyramid, triangular prism, and triangular pyramid, using the nets to find the surface area of these prisms.
description_plain: Your child can make nets (flat patterns) for rectangular and triangular prisms and pyramids and use them to find surface area.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas V = lwh and V = Bh to find the volume of right rectangular prisms with fractional edge lengths to solve real-world and mathematical problems, including solving for an unknown dimension.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find the volume of rectangular prisms with fractional edges and solve problems, including finding missing measurements.

subject: Math
description: Find the area of triangles, quadrilaterals, and polygons by composing or decomposing to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can find the area of triangles, four-sided shapes, and other polygons by breaking them apart or putting them together to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving area, volume, and surface area.
description_plain: Your child will solve problems that involve finding area, volume, and surface area of shapes.

subject: Math
description: Write, solve, and graph one-step inequalities in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can write, solve, and graph simple inequalities (like less than or greater than) to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Write and solve one-step equations in real-world and mathematical problems, involving positive rational numbers and zero.
description_plain: Your child can write and solve simple one-step equations using positive numbers and zero in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description:
Use substitution to determine if a given value in a specified set makes an equation or inequality true.
  • Include the following inequality symbols: <,>,≤,≥,≠
description_plain: Your child uses substitution to check if a number makes an equation or inequality true, including symbols like <, >, ≤, ≥, and ≠.

subject: Math
description: Focus on reasoning about and solving equations and inequalities.
description_plain: Your child focuses on thinking through and solving equations and inequalities.

subject: Math
description: Identify when two expressions are equivalent by using properties of operations including like terms.
description_plain: Your child can tell when two expressions are equal by using math rules like combining like terms.

subject: Math
description: Generate equivalent expressions by applying the associative, commutative, distributive, and identity properties.
description_plain: Your child can create equal expressions by using math properties like grouping, switching order, distributing, and identity.

subject: Math
description: Write and evaluate expressions for given values of variables, using order of operations, including expressions with whole number exponents.
description_plain: Your child can write and calculate expressions using the correct order of operations, including powers with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use mathematical terms to identify parts of an expression, including the names of operations, terms, factors, coefficients, variables, and constants.
description_plain: Your child uses math words to name parts of an expression, like operations, terms, factors, coefficients, variables, and constants.

subject: Math
description: Read and write expressions in real-world or mathematical problems in which letters stand for numbers.
description_plain: Your child can read and write expressions where letters stand for numbers in real-life or math problems.

subject: Math
description: Extend their understanding of arithmetic to algebraic expressions.
description_plain: Your child builds on their arithmetic skills to understand and work with algebra expressions.

subject: Math
description: Find a percent of a quantity as a rate per 100 and solve problems involving finding the whole when given a part and the percent.
description_plain: Your child can find a percent of a number and solve problems where they find the whole amount when given a part and the percent.

subject: Math
description: Create various representations to compare ratios and find missing values to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can make different pictures or charts to compare ratios and find missing numbers to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Give examples of unit rates as a ratio that compares two quantities with different units of measure, limited to non-complex fractions.
description_plain: Your child can give examples of unit rates that compare two different units, like miles per hour, using simple fractions.

subject: Math
description: Calculate unit rates to include unit pricing and constant speed.
description_plain: Your child can calculate unit rates, like price per item or speed, to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use precise ratio language and notation to describe a ratio as a relationship between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child uses clear ratio language and symbols to describe how two quantities relate to each other.

subject: Math
description: Understand ratio concepts and use proportional reasoning to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child understands ratios and uses them to solve problems by comparing amounts.

subject: Math
description: Use the distributive property and the greatest common factor to rewrite the sum of two whole numbers, 1 through 100.
description_plain: Your child uses the distributive property and finds the greatest common factor to rewrite sums of whole numbers from 1 to 100.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems with the least common multiple of two whole numbers less than or equal to 12.
description_plain: Your child solves problems using the least common multiple of two whole numbers up to 12.

subject: Math
description: Solve real-world and mathematical problems with the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than or equal to 100.
description_plain: Your child solves problems using the greatest common factor of two whole numbers up to 100.

subject: Math
description: Use factors and multiples to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child uses factors and multiples to help solve math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use any standard algorithm to fluently multiply and divide multi-digit decimals and fractions in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide decimals and fractions with many digits using standard methods to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Use any standard algorithm to fluently add and subtract multi-digit decimals and fractions in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract decimals and fractions with many digits using standard methods to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Divide multi-digit numbers fluently in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can divide large numbers fluently to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description:
Solve problems involving the division of fractions in real-world and mathematical problems.
  • Fractions include all forms of fractions.
description_plain: Your child solves problems that involve dividing all types of fractions in real-life and math situations.

subject: Math
description:
Interpret and represent quotients of fractions.
  • Fractions include all forms of fractions.
description_plain: Your child understands and shows how to divide fractions in different forms.

subject: Math
description: Extend previous knowledge of operations to decimals and fractions, involving positive rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child builds on what they know about math operations to work with decimals and fractions using positive numbers.

subject: Math
description: Convert between fractions, decimals, and percents in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can change numbers between fractions, decimals, and percents to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the absolute value of numbers for positive or negative quantities in a real-world context.
description_plain: Your child understands the absolute value of numbers as the distance from zero, whether the number is positive or negative, in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Compare rational numbers, using inequalities (<,>,≤,≥,≠) and order on a number line.
description_plain: Your child compares rational numbers using symbols like <, >, ≤, ≥, and ≠ and orders them on a number line.

subject: Math
description: Find and plot rational numbers on horizontal and vertical number lines in real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child finds and places rational numbers on horizontal and vertical number lines in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Explain positive and negative integers as being opposite values or directions and the meaning of 0 in a real-world context.
description_plain: Your child explains positive and negative integers as opposite values or directions and understands what zero means in real-life situations.

subject: Math
description: Use fractions, decimals, integers, and absolute values to represent real-world situations.
description_plain: Your child uses fractions, decimals, integers, and absolute values to represent and solve real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Use theoretical probability of an event in a simple experiment to predict the number of times that an event will occur for a large number of experiments.
description_plain: Your child uses the chance of an event happening to predict how many times it will happen in many tries.

subject: Math
description: Determine experimental probabilities in simple experiments and represent as fractions, decimals, and percents.
description_plain: Your child finds experimental probabilities from simple experiments and shows them as fractions, decimals, and percents.

subject: Math
description: Recognize that probabilities in a simple experiment can be qualitative descriptors of likelihood: impossible (0), unlikely, neither likely nor unlikely, likely, or certain (1).
description_plain: Your child understands that probabilities can describe how likely something is, like impossible, unlikely, likely, or certain.

subject: Math
description:
Determine the sample space of a simple experiment and use the sample space to determine the theoretical probability of a given set of outcomes.
  • Simple experiments include: tossing a fair coin, spinning a fair spinner, rolling a fair dice, picking a random marble from a bag, and selecting a random card from a deck
description_plain: Your child lists all possible outcomes of simple experiments like coin tosses or dice rolls and uses this to find the chance of certain results.

subject: Math
description: Understand theoretical and experimental probability for simple experiments.
description_plain: Your child understands both theoretical and experimental probability for simple experiments.

subject: Math
description: Use a random sampling of a population to draw valid inferences and generalizations of populations.
description_plain: Your child uses random samples from a group to make good guesses and conclusions about the whole group.

subject: Math
description: Distinguish between a random and non-random sample.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between a random sample and a non-random sample when collecting data.

subject: Math
description: Understand sampling and use samples to make inferences.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use samples to learn about a larger group.

subject: Math
description: Select an appropriate measure(s) of center or variability and draw valid comparative inferences for two data sets.
description_plain: Your child can choose the right way to compare two sets of data and explain the differences.

subject: Math
description:
Graph two numerical data sets and compare their variability.
  • Variability includes: range, interquartile range, or mean absolute deviation
description_plain: Your child can make graphs for two sets of numbers and compare how spread out the data is using measures like range or average distance.

subject: Math
description: Recognize, create, and interpret categorical data in a circle graph.
description_plain: Your child can create and understand circle graphs that show categories of data.

subject: Math
description: Interpret data displayed in a histogram and box plot to answer questions about the data.
description_plain: Your child can read histograms and box plots to answer questions about the data shown.

subject: Math
description: Interpret and organize data.
description_plain: Interpret and organize data.

subject: Math
description: Calculate the scale factor, compute the actual lengths from the scale in a drawing, and reproduce a scale drawing using another scale.
description_plain: Your child can find the scale factor in drawings, figure out real lengths from a scale, and make new scale drawings using a different scale.

subject: Math
description: Understand and use scale factor.
description_plain: Your child understands what a scale factor is and how to use it.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step problems involving supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles to include solving for an unknown angle in a figure.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems to find unknown angles using rules about supplementary, complementary, vertical, and adjacent angles.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems using various angle properties of lines.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems using different angle rules related to lines.

subject: Math
description:
Describe the two-dimensional figure that results from slicing a three-dimensional figure parallel and perpendicular to the base.
  • Three-dimensional figures include: right rectangular prisms, triangular prisms, and cylinders
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat shape made when slicing 3D shapes like rectangular prisms, triangular prisms, and cylinders either parallel or perpendicular to the base.

subject: Math
description: Describe cross sections of three-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can describe the cross sections made when cutting 3D shapes.

subject: Math
description: Apply the formulas for the volume and surface area of right rectangular prisms, rectangular pyramids, triangular prisms, and triangular pyramids to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas to find volume and surface area of shapes like rectangular prisms and pyramids to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Use area and circumference formulas of a circle to solve real-world and mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can use formulas for the area and circumference of circles to solve real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Describe the proportional relationship between the circumference and diameter of a circle.
description_plain: Your child understands how the circumference and diameter of a circle are related in a proportional way.

subject: Math
description: Solve problems involving area, volume, and surface area.
description_plain: Your child can solve problems involving area, volume, and surface area of shapes.

subject: Math
description: Describe the relationship between the dependent and independent variables in an equation using tables and graphs, relating these to the equation.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the dependent and independent variables relate in an equation by using tables and graphs.

subject: Math
description: Write an equation to express two quantities in terms of the dependent and independent variables.
description_plain: Your child can write an equation that shows how two quantities depend on each other.

subject: Math
description: Use understanding of algebraic expressions and equations to represent relationships between two quantities.
description_plain: Your child can use algebra expressions and equations to show how two quantities are connected.

subject: Math
description: Create, solve, and graph two-step inequalities in real-world and mathematical problems in the forms px ± q > r, px ± q < r, px ± q ≥ r, and px ± q ≤ r.
description_plain: Your child can create, solve, and graph two-step inequalities to solve real-world and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Model and solve fluently two-step equations in real-world or mathematical problems.
description_plain: Your child can model and solve two-step equations in real-life and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply previous knowledge of equations and inequalities to two-step problems.
description_plain: Your child can use what they know about equations and inequalities to solve two-step problems.

subject: Math
description: Generate and justify equivalent expressions, using properties of operations to add, subtract, factor, and expand linear expressions with rational coefficients within mathematical and real-world problems.
description_plain: Your child can create and explain equivalent expressions by adding, subtracting, factoring, and expanding using properties of operations.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations to create equivalent expressions.
description_plain: Your child can use operation rules to make equivalent expressions.

subject: Math
description: Given a graph with a proportional relationship, explain the meaning of a point (x,y) on the graph, including the origin (0,0) and the unit rate (1,r).
description_plain: Your child can explain what points on a graph of a proportional relationship mean, including the origin and the unit rate.

subject: Math
description: Create equations in the form of y = mx from tables, verbal descriptions, or graphs.
description_plain: Your child can write equations like y = mx from tables, words, or graphs.

subject: Math
description: Compare two different proportional relationships represented in different forms.
description_plain: Your child can compare two proportional relationships shown in different ways.

subject: Math
description: Determine whether two quantities represent proportional relationships by using equivalent ratios in a table and by graphing on a coordinate plane.
description_plain: Your child can decide if two quantities are proportional by using tables and graphs.

subject: Math
description: Analyze proportional relationships and solve multi-step ration and percent problems.
description_plain: Your child can analyze proportional relationships and solve multi-step problems involving ratios and percents.

subject: Math
description: Solve multi-step ratio and percent problems in a real-world context, including percent error and percent increase and decrease.
description_plain: Your child can solve multi-step real-world problems involving ratios and percents, including percent error and percent increase or decrease.

subject: Math
description: Calculate unit rates in real-world contexts that include complex fractions.
description_plain: Your child can find unit rates in real-life problems, even when the numbers are complex fractions.

subject: Math
description: Determine the unit rate (constant of proportionality) from tables, graphs, equations, diagrams, or verbal descriptions of proportional relationships.
description_plain: Your child can find the unit rate from tables, graphs, equations, pictures, or descriptions of proportional relationships.

subject: Math
description: Analyze and use unit rates to solve problems.
description_plain: Your child can use unit rates to solve problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply operations with rational numbers involving the order of operations, involving nested grouping symbols.
description_plain: Your child can do operations with rational numbers following the correct order, even when there are many grouping symbols.

subject: Math
description: Use multiplication and division with rational numbers in any form to solve multi-step problems in real-world and mathematical contexts.
description_plain: Your child can multiply and divide rational numbers in any form to solve multi-step problems in real life and math.

subject: Math
description: Use addition and subtraction with rational numbers in any form to solve multi-step problems in real-world and mathematical contexts.
description_plain: Your child can add and subtract rational numbers in any form to solve multi-step real-world and math problems.

subject: Math
description: Apply properties of operations as strategies to fluently add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use operation rules to add, subtract, multiply, and divide rational numbers easily.

subject: Math
description: Apply all properties and operations to all rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use all operation rules with all rational numbers.

subject: Math
description: Interpret the products and quotients of rational numbers by describing real-world contexts.
description_plain: Your child can explain what multiplying and dividing rational numbers means in real-world situations.

subject: Math
description: Convert a rational number in fraction form to decimal form and recognize that the decimal form of a rational number terminates in 0s or eventually repeats.
description_plain: Your child can change fractions into decimals and knows that decimals for rational numbers either end or repeat.

subject: Math
description: Demonstrate in real-world contexts the distance between two rational numbers on the number line as the absolute value of their differences.
description_plain: Your child can find the distance between two numbers on a number line by subtracting them and taking the positive value.

subject: Math
description: Model and describe additive inverse in real-world situations to show opposite quantities combine to make 0.
description_plain: Your child understands that adding opposite numbers, like +3 and -3, together equals zero, and can explain this with real-life examples.

subject: Math
description: Represent addition and subtraction of rational numbers in real-world contexts using a variety of forms.
description_plain: Your child can show adding and subtracting fractions or decimals using different ways and real-world problems.

subject: Math
description: Model and compute with rational numbers.
description_plain: Your child can use models and calculations to work with fractions, decimals, and negative numbers.

subject: Math
description: Use theoretical probability of an event in a compound experiment to predict the number of times that event will occur for a large number of experiments.
description_plain: Your child can use probability to predict how often an event will happen when doing many trials.

subject: Math
description: Determine theoretical and experimental probabilities of compound experiments.
description_plain: Your child can find the chance of events happening by comparing expected results with actual results from experiments.

subject: Math
description:
Determine the sample space and use the sample space to determine the theoretical probability of a given set of outcomes for compound experiments, using organized lists, tables, or tree diagrams.
  • Compound experiments include a combination of two different experiments.
description_plain: Your child can list all possible outcomes of combined experiments and use that list to find the chance of certain results.

subject: Math
description: Understand theoretical and experimental probability for compound experiments using organized lists, tables, or tree diagrams.
description_plain: Your child understands how to find and compare expected and actual chances of events happening in combined experiments using lists or diagrams.

subject: Math
description: Construct and interpret a relative frequency table, using data from two categorical variables collected from the same subject.
description_plain: Your child can make and read tables that show how often different pairs of categories happen together.

subject: Math
description: Construct straight lines to approximately fit data displaying a linear association when presented in scatter plots.
description_plain: Your child can draw a straight line that best fits points on a graph when the points show a clear pattern.

subject: Math
description: Construct scatter plots using bivariate data; determine if the data displays a linear or nonlinear pattern and positive, negative, or no association.
description_plain: Your child can make scatter plots from two sets of data and decide if the points form a straight line or another pattern, and whether the relationship is positive, negative, or none.

subject: Math
description: Investigate patterns of association to bivariate data.
description_plain: Your child explores how two sets of data relate to each other by looking for patterns.

subject: Math
description: Given two similar two-dimensional figures, describe a sequence of transformations that exhibits similarity, including rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how to show two similar shapes are alike by turning, flipping, sliding, or resizing them.

subject: Math
description:
Describe the effects of rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations on two-dimensional figures using coordinates.
  • Rotations include: 90°, 180°, and 270°
description_plain: Your child can describe how turning (90°, 180°, 270°), flipping, sliding, or resizing shapes changes their position on a grid.

subject: Math
description:
Perform a given sequence of transformations of a figure on the coordinate plane, including rotations, reflections, translations, and dilations.
  • Rotations include: 90°, 180°, and 270°
description_plain: Your child can move shapes on a grid by turning (90°, 180°, 270°), flipping, sliding, or resizing them in a given order.

subject: Math
description: Given two congruent figures, describe a sequence of transformations that maps one figure to another.
description_plain: Your child can explain how to move one shape to exactly match another shape using turns, flips, or slides.

subject: Math
description:
Identify a single transformation used to transform one figure onto another on a coordinate plane.
  • Rotations include: 90°, 180°, and 270°
description_plain: Your child can identify one way a shape was moved on a grid, like a turn (90°, 180°, 270°), flip, or slide, to match another shape.

subject: Math
description: Given a figure, draw a congruent figure on a coordinate plane resulting from a rotation, reflection, or translation.
description_plain: Your child can draw a shape on a grid that is the same size and shape as another, after turning, flipping, or sliding it.

subject: Math
description: Use concrete models, diagrams, or geometry to understand congruence and similarity.
description_plain: Your child uses models and pictures to understand when shapes are exactly the same or just similar.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points in a coordinate system.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points on a grid.

subject: Math
description: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles.
description_plain: Your child can use the Pythagorean Theorem to find missing side lengths in right triangles.

subject: Math
description: Model or explain an informal proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and its converse.
description_plain: Your child can explain or show a simple proof of the Pythagorean Theorem and how it works backwards.

subject: Math
description: Explore right triangles and apply the Pythagorean Theorem.
description_plain: Your child explores right triangles and uses the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems.

subject: Math
description:

subject: Math
description: Describe cross sections of three-dimensional figures.
description_plain: Your child can describe the flat cross sections made when cutting 3D shapes.

subject: Math

subject: Math

subject: Math
description: Solve systems of equations.

description: Simplify and perform operations with radical expressions without variables; rationalizing denominators should not include conjugates.
description_plain: Your child can simplify and work with radical expressions without variables and can make denominators simpler without using conjugates.

subject: Math
description: Simplify and perform operations with radical expressions without variables; rationalizing denominators should not include conjugates.
description_plain: Your child can simplify and work with radical expressions without variables and can make denominators simpler without using conjugates.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; compare the system of polynomials to the system of integers when performing operations.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and understand how these operations are like working with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; compare the system of polynomials to the system of integers when performing operations.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and understand how these operations are like working with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; compare the system of polynomials to the system of integers when performing operations.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and understand how these operations are like working with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Add, subtract, and multiply polynomials; compare the system of polynomials to the system of integers when performing operations.
description_plain: Your child can add, subtract, and multiply polynomials and understand how these operations are like working with whole numbers.

subject: Math
description: Students simplify algebraic and numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can simplify both algebraic and numerical expressions.

subject: Math
description: Students simplify algebraic and numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can simplify both algebraic and numerical expressions.

subject: Math
description: Students simplify algebraic and numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can simplify both algebraic and numerical expressions.

subject: Math
description: Students simplify algebraic and numerical expressions.
description_plain: Your child can simplify both algebraic and numerical expressions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine reasons for and effects of social, economic, political, and cultural changes during the 1920s, including the Harlem Renaissance, Great Migration, Prohibition, and financial and consumer trends such as the rise of the automobile, buying on credit, advertising, household products, sports, and the arts.
description_plain: Your child can explore why and how society, economy, politics, and culture changed in the 1920s, including events like the Harlem Renaissance and the rise of cars and advertising.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the effects of key political issues of the 1910s and 1920s:
  • Nativism and cultural assimilation: (e.g., Emergency Quota Act, Sacco and Vanzetti)
  • Racial discrimination and civil rights: (e.g., continued presence of Ku Klux Klan, founding of NAACP, Elaine Race Massacre, Tulsa Race Massacre)
  • Indigenous rights: (e.g., Indian Citizenship Act, United States vs. Winans)
  • Rise of Communism/Marxism: (e.g., First Red Scare, J. Edgar Hoover)
description_plain: Your child can understand the effects of important political issues in the 1910s and 1920s, like immigration limits, racial discrimination, Indigenous rights, and fears of communism.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze short- and long-term effects of Progressive Era reforms at the local, state, and national levels:
  • Food safety laws (e.g., Pure Food and Drug Act, Meat Packing Act)
  • Industry and labor regulations (e.g., bans against child labor, Sherman Anti-Trust Act, Clayton Anti-Trust Act)
  • Progressive legislation (e.g., adoption of initiative, referendum, recall, 16th, 17th, 18th, and 19th Amendments)
  • Social and labor movements (e.g., populism, civil service reform, temperance, the Granger Movement)
description_plain: Your child can analyze how Progressive Era reforms changed laws about food safety, labor, government processes, and social movements.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Investigate the impact of Progressive Era reformers and ideas:
  • Booker T. Washington vs. W.E.B. DuBois on civil rights
  • John Dewey on education
  • Jane Addams on social work
  • Rise of religious liberalism, Social Darwinism, and the eugenics movement
description_plain: Your child can learn about important Progressive Era reformers and ideas, including leaders in civil rights, education, social work, and social theories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the changing role of the United States in the world from 1890-1930 and the effects on future eras.
description_plain: Your child can study how the United States' role in the world changed between 1890 and 1930 and how this affected later times.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Investigate political, social, and technological outcomes of World War I on American society:
  • New weapons in warfare
  • Women entering the workforce
  • Espionage and Sedition Acts
  • Wilson's Fourteen Points and the ratification debate of the Treaty of Versailles
description_plain: Your child can investigate how World War I affected American society, including new weapons, women working, laws about spying, and peace efforts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze motives for and significance of America's entry into World War I, including the sinking of the RMS Lusitania, the Zimmerman Telegram, unrestricted submarine warfare, the Argonne Offensive, and key military leaders (e.g., General John J. Pershing and Alvin York).
description_plain: Your child can analyze why the U.S. entered World War I, including events like the sinking of the Lusitania and important military leaders.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain the origins, development, and impact of American expansionism, including the geographic effects of acquiring new territories, the expansionist foreign policy under William McKinley, Theodore Roosevelt, and William Taft, and the role of the Spanish-American War:
  • Annexation of Alaska, Hawaii, Panama Canal
  • Acquisition of federally protected land such as national parks
  • Role of yellow journalism
  • Treaty of Paris: annexation of Philippines, Puerto Rico, and Guam
  • Open Door Policy with East Asia
description_plain: Your child can explain how and why America expanded its territory around 1890-1900s, including wars, new lands, and policies with other countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate federal policy toward Indigenous nations, westward expansion, and the resulting struggles and issues that arose.
description_plain: Your child can evaluate how federal policies affected Indigenous nations and westward expansion, including the problems that came from these actions.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine government policies and laws that addressed the escalating labor conflicts and the creation, purpose, and rise of labor unions:
  • Factory system
  • Immigrant and child labor
  • Convict leasing
  • Union tactics (e.g., strikes, negotiations)
description_plain: Your child can examine government laws and policies about labor conflicts and how labor unions formed and worked.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the historical significance of the women's suffrage movement, including key individuals, groups, and events that contributed:
  • Individuals: (e.g., Susan B. Anthony, Elizabeth Cady Stanton, Sojourner Truth, Ida Wells, Grimké sisters)
  • Groups: abolition and temperance movements groups
  • Events: (e.g., passage of married women's property acts in states, Seneca Falls Convention, passage of the 19th Amendment)
description_plain: Your child can analyze the women's suffrage movement, including important people, groups, and events that helped women gain voting rights.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the causes and effects of immigration after 1870, including push-pull factors, ethnic enclaves, the assimilation process, and rise of nativism through law (e.g., Chinese Exclusion Act, Johnson-Reed Act).
description_plain: Your child can study immigration after 1870, including reasons people moved, immigrant communities, how they adapted, and laws against some groups.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze economic, geographic, and technological growth associated with the Second Industrial Revolution and its impact on American society:
  • Entrepreneurship: (e.g., John Rockefeller, Andrew Carnegie, J.P. Morgan, Henry Ford, Cornelius Vanderbilt, Jay Gould, Madam C.J. Walker)
  • Energy: (e.g., oil, electricity, inventions/discoveries by Thomas Edison and Nikola Tesla)
  • Transportation: (e.g., growth of railroads and steel, Wright brothers, automobile and assembly line)
description_plain: Your child can analyze how the Second Industrial Revolution changed the economy, technology, and society, including famous entrepreneurs and inventions.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate political, social, and economic impacts of Reconstruction:
  • Political: (e.g., black voting rights, African-American role in government, military occupation of Southern states, Freedmen's Bureau, Reconstruction Amendments, election of Rutherford B. Hayes, restoration of Confederate voting rights, subsequent passage of Jim Crow laws and Black Codes)
  • Social: (e.g., public education and dramatic increase of literacy rates, rise of the Ku Klux Klan)
  • Economic: (e.g., sharecropping system and crop liens, African-American economic positions, Southern economy)
description_plain: Your child can evaluate the political, social, and economic effects of Reconstruction after the Civil War, including voting rights, education, and economic systems.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the historical significance of key Civil War battles, events, and people:
  • Battles: Fort Sumter, Bull Run, Gettysburg, Sherman's March, Shiloh, Vicksburg, Appomattox
  • Events: Lincoln presidency, Emancipation Proclamation, Gettysburg Address, Second Inaugural Address, assassination, Sultana disaster
  • People: various Union and Confederate leaders and generals
description_plain: Your child can analyze important Civil War battles, events, and leaders, including key speeches and outcomes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze political, social, and economic effects of the Civil War on America including destruction of property and infrastructure, a weakened economy, a stronger federal government, and loss of life and livelihoods.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Civil War affected America by causing damage to buildings and land, hurting the economy, making the federal government stronger, and causing many deaths and hardships.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain ways economic development of the North and South created certain advantages and disadvantages during the course of the Civil War:
  • Union/Northern states: (e.g., largely industrialized, manufacturing, extensive railroad network)
  • Confederacy/Southern states: (e.g., largely agricultural, dependence on chattel slavery, less extensive transportation system)
description_plain: Your child understands how the North’s factories and railroads helped it during the Civil War, while the South’s farms and reliance on slavery created different strengths and challenges.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Develop historical arguments and explanations of causes of the Civil War:
  • Role of Congress and states' rights (e.g., power to admit new states, tariff debate, Supremacy Clause, nullification crisis)
  • Debates on the issue of slavery (e.g., expansion vs. popular sovereignty vs. free soil anti-slavery vs. total abolition)
  • Sectionalism (e.g., cultural and economic differences between the North and South)
description_plain: Your child can explain the main reasons the Civil War started, including conflicts over states’ rights, slavery, and differences between the North and South.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the historical significance of key individuals, groups, and events leading to the Civil War
  • Individuals: (e.g., Henry Clay, John C. Calhoun, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Beecher Stowe, Abraham Lincoln)
  • Groups: (e.g., Free-Soil Party, Republican Party, Northern and Southern Democrats)
  • Events: (e.g., John Brown's raid, Nat Turner's rebellion, Underground Railroad, election of Abraham Lincoln, state secessionist conventions)
description_plain: Your child can describe important people, groups, and events that led to the Civil War, like Abraham Lincoln, the Free-Soil Party, and John Brown’s raid.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate key laws and decisions addressing the issue of slavery prior to the Civil War:
  • Missouri Compromise on the admittance of free and slave states
  • Compromise of 1850, including the Fugitive Slave Act, and its impact on the abolition movement
  • Popular sovereignty movement and Bleeding Kansas
  • Dred Scott vs. Sanford decision
description_plain: Your child understands key laws and court decisions about slavery before the Civil War, such as the Missouri Compromise and the Dred Scott case.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the development of regional tensions prior to the Civil War:
  • Economic development: (e.g., effects of cotton gin invention, Industrial Revolution, growth of manufacturing and railroads in northern states)
  • Political actions: (e.g., tariffs, nullification crisis, compromises, disenfranchisement of free blacks)
  • Expansion of slavery, immigration, and westward migration
description_plain: Your child can explain how economic growth, political actions, and the spread of slavery and immigration caused tensions between regions before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate proper etiquette for interacting with the Arkansas and American flags and evaluate the use of various flags between the 1850 and 1930 (e.g., flags used in the Civil War, development of the Arkansas flag).
description_plain: Your child knows how to show respect when handling the Arkansas and American flags and can talk about different flags used between 1850 and 1930.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from Civil War and Reconstruction, 1850-1877 (Era 5), to the Emergence of a Modern America, 1890-1930 (Era 7). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands important times in U.S. history from the Civil War through early 1900s and how people’s lives and views changed during those years.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the effects of the Mexican-American War on the United States, including new territory for the United States and expansion of slavery.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Mexican-American War gave the U.S. new land and affected the spread of slavery.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze major purposes, implementation, and effects of public policies during the Jacksonian presidency, including Jacksonian Democracy, veto of the National Bank, the spoils system, Indian Removal Act, and the Trail of Tears.
description_plain: Your child understands important policies during President Jackson’s time, like removing Native Americans and changing the National Bank.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the development of regional tensions prior to the Civil War:
  • Economic development: (e.g., effects of cotton gin invention, Industrial Revolution, growth of manufacturing and railroads in northern states)
  • Political actions: (e.g., tariffs, nullification crisis, compromises, disenfranchisement of free blacks)
  • Expansion of slavery, immigration, and westward migration
description_plain: Your child can explain how economic growth, political events, and the spread of slavery and immigration caused regional conflicts before the Civil War.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the concept of Manifest Destiny and the factors that affected territorial expansion and the development of the nation. Events may include:
  • Louisiana Purchase
  • Monroe Doctrine
  • Adams-Onís Treaty
  • Development of the Erie Canal
  • Oregon Trail
  • Santa Fe Trail
  • California Gold Rush
  • Panic of 1837
  • Rising conflict between settlers and Indigenous groups
description_plain: Your child understands the idea of Manifest Destiny and important events that helped the U.S. grow, like the Louisiana Purchase and the Oregon Trail.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the impact and significance of the War of 1812, including British impressment of American sailors, the role of James and Dolley Madison, and various battles that contributed to an American identity (e.g., Battle of Fort McHenry and "The Star Spangled Banner," Battle of New Orleans).
description_plain: Your child can describe how the War of 1812 affected America, including important battles and how it helped create a sense of American pride.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate how early Presidents and historical events influenced the development of the new nation:
  • Establishment of Washington, D.C., as nation's capital
  • Washington's Farewell Address
  • Rise of political parties
  • Jeffersonian democracy and the Louisiana Purchase
  • Judicial power defined through Marbury vs. Madison
  • Banking and tariff policy on the national economy
description_plain: Your child understands how early presidents and events shaped the new nation, including the capital city, political parties, and important court cases.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine short- and long-term effects of the drafting and signing of the U.S. Constitution:
  • Federalism and the establishment of three branches
  • Bicameral house (i.e., the Great Compromise)
  • National powers (e.g., coining money, raising an army, power to tax)
  • Enumeration of individual rights (e.g., Bill of Rights)
  • Amendment process (i.e., design, purpose, and difficulty)
description_plain: Your child can explain the effects of the U.S. Constitution, like how the government is divided, the rights people have, and how changes can be made.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the effects of the American Revolutionary War on the newly formed United States and the rest of the world:
  • National effects: (e.g., vulnerable young nation, colonial independence, creation of the Articles of Confederation expansion of territory via Treaty of Paris, destruction of farmland, significant casualties, loss of life/livelihood, inflation, scarcity of resources)
  • International effects: (e.g., international relations, catalyst for international revolutions, loyalists relocation to Canada, interruption of global trade)
description_plain: Your child understands how the American Revolution affected the new country and the world, including changes in land, government, and international relations.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the significance of various battles and military leaders during the American Revolution:
  • Battles: Lexington and Concord, Bunker Hill, Saratoga, Yorktown
  • Military leaders/groups: George Washington, John Paul Jones, Nathanael Greene, First Rhode Island Regiment
description_plain: Your child can describe important battles and leaders from the American Revolution, like George Washington and the Battle of Yorktown.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the significance of the Declaration of Independence, including key political concepts of natural rights, natural law, social contract theory, right to revolution, right to self-determination, and tyranny.
description_plain: Your child understands the Declaration of Independence and ideas like natural rights and the right to change a government that is unfair.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate how individuals and groups influenced the American Revolutionary movement:
  • Writers & influencers: (e.g., Thomas Paine's "Common Sense," Patrick Henry's "Give Me Liberty" speech, Benjamin Franklin's Join or Die cartoon, Phillis Wheatley, Abigail Adams, Haym Salomon)
  • Military & political: (e.g., George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, Paul Revere, Salem Poor, Peter Salem, Deborah Sampson, Mary Ludgwig Hays, James Armistead)
  • Groups: (e.g., Culper Spy Ring, Sons and Daughters of Liberty)
description_plain: Your child can explain how different people and groups, like writers and soldiers, helped the American Revolution succeed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the Revolutionary movement from the perspective of various groups, which may include patriots, loyalists, British and French leaders, Indigenous people, and indentured or enslaved Americans.
description_plain: Your child understands the American Revolution from different viewpoints, including patriots, loyalists, Indigenous peoples, and others.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze causes and ideas leading to the American Revolution:
  • French and Indian War
  • Stamp Act, Townshend Duties, and the Intolerable Acts
  • Boston Tea Party
  • Colonial self-government, representation, and individual liberty
description_plain: Your child can explain the causes of the American Revolution, like unfair taxes, protests like the Boston Tea Party, and ideas about freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Research the development of the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies through inquiry and questioning. Inquiry questions may include:
  • Why did people settle where they did?
  • How did they solve problems?
  • Was life better in the colonies than in England?
  • Was life better in some colonies than others?
  • How were patterns of settlement influenced by beliefs, economics, and geography?
description_plain: Your child researches why people settled in different colonies, how they solved problems, and how life in the colonies compared to England.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the economic and cultural effects of indentured servitude and slavery in the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies:
  • Conditions of the Middle Passage
  • Life and experiences of those who were enslaved versus those who were indentured
  • Growth and economic dependence on slavery, especially in Southern colonies
  • Early attempts at abolition (e.g., Rhode Island in 1652, Vermont in 1777, Quaker Abolition movement)
description_plain: Your child understands how indentured servants and enslaved people lived and worked in the colonies and how slavery grew, especially in the South.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate short- and long-term effects of European exploration and settlement ventures in the Americas and Arkansas, including why some experienced hardship and failure. Reasons may include:
  • Conflict with other nations (including Indigenous nations)
  • Disease
  • Poor climate conditions
  • Economic issues such as the failure of the common store system in Jamestown and Plymouth Colony
description_plain: Your child can explain how European explorers and settlers faced challenges like conflicts, disease, and bad weather in the Americas.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine economic, political, and religious reasons for European exploration in the Americas:
  • Trade and trade routes
  • Wealth
  • Colonies for empire expansion
  • Religious influence and freedom
description_plain: Your child understands why Europeans explored the Americas, including for trade, wealth, land, and religious freedom.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Research and compare cultural characteristics of major pre-Columbian Indigenous societies within North America through a variety of artwork, journals, archaeological findings, and other artifacts. Pre-Columbian Indigenous societies might include:
  • Mesoamerican groups (e.g., Olmec, Maya, Aztec)
  • Mound builders
  • Cliff dwellers
  • Southwest
  • Great Plains
  • Pacific Northwest
  • Woodland peoples
  • Indigenous Peoples of the Arctic and Subarctic
  • Mississippians in Arkansas
description_plain: Your child researches and compares the cultures of Native American groups before Europeans arrived, using art and artifacts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the United States' Beginnings (Era 1) through 1850 (Era 4). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands important U.S. history from the country’s beginnings through 1850, including how society, economy, and politics changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze cooperation among the colonies and Indigenous communities during and after natural and human-made disasters such as the French and Indian War, American Revolution, disease outbreaks, famine, and weather phenomena.
description_plain: Your child can explain how colonies and Native communities worked together during disasters like wars, disease outbreaks, and famines.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine reasons for population shifts in early America and the effects on various regions:
  • European immigration and colonization
  • Diseases in both colonial and Indigenous populations
  • Indentured servitude
  • Transatlantic slave trade
description_plain: Your child understands why people moved to early America and how immigration, disease, and slavery affected different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the relationships between American colonists and various Indigenous societies, considering the location and use of natural resources and changes to the physical environment. This may include pelt and fur trading, large-scale farming, use of timber, increased resource consumption, hunting and fishing, and differences on the view of land ownership.
description_plain: Your child can describe how American colonists and Native peoples interacted, including trading and using natural resources differently.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze ways cultural and environmental characteristics influenced population, settlement, and movement of goods in the American colonies:
  • Cultural (e.g., religious affiliations, language and ethnic groups, locations of various Indigenous nations)
  • Economic (e.g., sources of labor)
  • Geographic (e.g., environmental characteristics, climate, topography, soil type, water access, etc.)
  • Governance (e.g., royal, charter, proprietary)
description_plain: Your child understands how culture, economy, geography, and government affected where people lived and how goods moved in the colonies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Synthesize information from a variety of sources to construct maps and other geographic representations of the United States from the original thirteen colonies to 1850.
description_plain: Your child can use different sources to create maps showing the United States from the first colonies up to 1850.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze human movement and economic activities over time using maps and other geographic representations:
  • Pre-Columbian Indigenous Nations' settlement patterns
  • Triangular trade and other trade routes
  • Columbian exchange
  • Regions of European exploration
  • Colonial settlement
description_plain: Your child understands how people moved and traded over time, including Native settlement patterns and early trade routes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe locations, environmental characteristics, and cultures of pre-Columbian Indigenous populations using maps and geographic representations.
description_plain: Your child can describe where Native American groups lived before Europeans arrived, using maps and other tools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to understand, analyze, and explain human interaction with each other and with the environment. This includes the spatial characteristics and patterns of human settlement and connections between global regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how maps and other tools help us learn about where people live and how they interact with the environment and each other.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain effects of increasing economic interdependence between the United States and other nations and regions:
  • Growth: (e.g., First Industrial Revolution, Louisiana Purchase, population growth, westward expansion, triangular trade, increased wealth, cooperation with other nations)
  • Conflict: (e.g., competition with other nations, Barbary Wars, Tecumseh's War, War of 1812)
description_plain: Your child can explain how the U.S. became more connected with other countries through trade and growth, and how this sometimes caused conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the effects of war and conflict on the economy of the United States from the colonial period to the early 1800s, including government printing of money, inflation, scarcity of resources, and smuggling.
description_plain: Your child understands how wars and conflicts affected the U.S. economy, including money problems and shortages.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain ways state and federal governments paid for the goods and services they provided through the early 1800s:
  • Taxation to provide for an army
  • Postal services
  • Building of roads
  • Tariffs
description_plain: Your child can explain how early governments paid for things like the army, mail, roads, and taxes.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Discuss roles of early financial institutions on the economy of the United States:
  • National Bank by Alexander Hamilton
  • Jackson's veto of the National Bank
  • State banks
description_plain: Your child understands the role of early banks in the U.S. economy, including the National Bank and state banks.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the forms and purposes of currency in early America through the Revolutionary period:
  • Continental currency vs. state currency
  • Early forms of currency in Indigenous nations
description_plain: Your child can explain different types of money used in early America, including paper money during the Revolution and Native currency.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the development of a free market system within the thirteen colonies and early United States:
  • Mercantilism
  • Property rights
  • Free enterprise
description_plain: Your child can explain how the free market system started in the thirteen colonies and early United States by learning about mercantilism, property rights, and free enterprise.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the economic characteristics of the colonial regions (New England, Middle, and Southern), including their diverse labor force, use and trade-offs of natural resources, and human and capital resources used to deliver goods and services.
description_plain: Your child will explore the different economies of the New England, Middle, and Southern colonies, including their workers, natural resources, and how they made and traded goods.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze historical developments in the interdependent system of commerce between the thirteen colonies, Great Britain, and the rest of the world:
  • European exploration and colonization of the Americas
  • Taxation (e.g., Stamp Act, Townshend Duties)
  • Conflict (e.g., French and Indian War, American Revolution)
  • Trade (e.g., triangular trade, Columbian Exchange, Navigation Act of 1651)
description_plain: Your child can study how trade and events like exploration, taxes, wars, and trade routes connected the thirteen colonies, Great Britain, and other countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes the exchange of goods and services; role of producers, consumers, and government in the marketplace; and growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how people make economic choices, how goods and services are exchanged, and the roles of producers, consumers, and government in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain the development of policies under the Articles of Confederation to address public problems after the Revolutionary War:
  • Northwest Ordinance (1787)
  • Articles' weaknesses
  • Shay's Rebellion
description_plain: Your child can explain how early government rules after the Revolutionary War, like the Northwest Ordinance, tried to solve problems and why the Articles of Confederation had weaknesses.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate ways rules and laws change society and reasons why people repeal or amend them at local, state, and federal levels:
  • Article V of the U.S. Constitution
  • Arkansas Constitution voter-approved amendment process
  • Repeal process
description_plain: Your child will learn how laws and rules change society and why people change or remove them at local, state, and national levels.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare processes for creating rules and laws at the local, state, and federal levels such as city council, county quorum court, Arkansas General Assembly, and Congress.
description_plain: Your child can compare how laws are made in local, state, and national governments, like city councils and Congress.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine historical developments that contributed to the rise of civic virtues in the United States, including colonial self-government and individual liberties. Some notable developments that contributed to the rise of self-government include:
  • Mayflower Compact
  • Colonial charters
  • House of Burgesses (Virginia)
  • Great Awakening
  • Salutary neglect
  • Trial of John Peter Zenger
  • Influence of the Iroquoi Confederacy/Six Nations
description_plain: Your child will study important events and ideas, like the Mayflower Compact and colonial self-government, that helped Americans develop good citizenship and freedoms.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze rights, responsibilities, and privileges of citizens and non-citizens in the United States:
  • 14th Amendment
  • 15th Amendment
  • 19th Amendment
  • 26th Amendment
description_plain: Your child understands the rights and duties of citizens and non-citizens in the U.S., including important changes from amendments like the 14th, 15th, 19th, and 26th.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate proper etiquette for interacting with the Arkansas and American flags and evaluate the use of various flags during the early American period (e.g., Betsy Ross flag, Flag of the Green Mountain Boys, First Navy Jack, Great Garrison Flag during War of 1812).
description_plain: Your child can show respect for the Arkansas and U.S. flags and learn about different flags used during early American history.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate various ways early historical figures fostered American citizenship and civic virtues. Historical figures may include:
  • Phillis Wheatley
  • James Armistead
  • Peter Salem
  • George Washington
  • John Adams
  • Abigail Adams
  • Salem Poor
  • Thomas Jefferson
  • Thomas Paine
  • Patrick Henry
  • Alexis de Tocqueville
description_plain: Your child will learn how early American leaders, like George Washington and Abigail Adams, helped build good citizenship and civic values.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine how the 9th and 10th Amendments entrust social organizations and institutions (e.g., individuals, families, religious/secular groups) with unenumerated rights not listed in the Constitution. Unenumerated rights may include:

  • Right to an education

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine various ways African Americans participated in the Civil Rights Movement and the effects of their actions:
  • Boycotts: (e.g., Montgomery bus boycott, Mississippi Freedom Summer)
  • Marches: (e.g., Birmingham, Selma, March on Washington)
  • Music: (e.g., "People Get Ready," "We Shall Overcome," "Freedom Highway," "Say It Loud - I'm Black and I'm Proud," "A Change is Gonna Come")
  • Sit-ins: (e.g. Greensboro, Nashville, University of Chicago, Baltimore)
  • Speeches: (e.g., "Letter from a Birmingham Jail," "I have a Dream," Fannie Lou Hamer's Testimony, Kwame Ture on "Black Power," Ella Jo Baker on the struggle for Civil Rights)
description_plain: Your child examines how African Americans took part in the Civil Rights Movement through boycotts, marches, music, sit-ins, and speeches, and what effects these actions had.

  • Speeches: (e.g., "Letter from a Birmingham Jail," "I have a Dream," Fannie Lou Hamer's Testimony, Kwame Ture on "Black Power," Ella Jo Baker on the struggle for Civil Rights)
description_plain: Your child examines how African Americans took part in the Civil Rights Movement through boycotts, marches, music, sit-ins, and speeches, and what effects these actions had.

  • Education and thought leaders: (e.g., Langston Hughes, Thomas Sowell, Zora Neale Hurston)

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze various experiences and responses to challenges of African American men and women from post-Reconstruction through the Jim Crow time period and early 20th century:
  • Art and entertainment: (e.g., Harlem Renaissance)
  • Education: (e.g., rising literacy rates, establishment of historically black colleges and universities, Booker T. Washington, W.E.B. Du Bois, Joseph Albert Booker)
  • Entrepreneurship: (e.g., Samuel T. Wilcox, Robert Gordon, Annie Malone, Frederick and Charles Patterson, Maggie Lena Walker, Madam C. J. Walker)
  • Religion: (e.g., growth of African Methodist Episcopal denomination [AME] and establishment of National Baptist Convention)
  • Political organizations and affiliations
  • Segregation and discrimination: (e.g., Jim Crow laws, Plessy vs. Ferguson, "sundown towns," lynchings)
description_plain: Your child examines how African Americans faced challenges and responded through art, education, business, religion, politics, and dealing with segregation from post-Reconstruction to early 1900s.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the responses of free and enslaved African American men and women to social, economic, and political conditions in different regions between 1820 and 1877. Responses may include:
  • Resistance and escape: (e.g., Underground Railroad, Harriet Tubman, Robert Smalls, West Bogan)
  • Rebellions and uprisings: (e.g., Nat Turner, Gabriel Prosser)
  • Calls for abolition and equality: (e.g., Frederick Douglass, Frances Harper, Ida B. Wells)
  • Court cases: (e.g., Dred Scott, Homer Plessy)
  • Back-to-Africa movement (e.g., Paul Cuffe, Martin Delany)
  • Political involvement and representation (e.g., Alexander Twilight, Hiram Rhodes Revels, Joseph Rainey)
description_plain: Your child studies how free and enslaved African Americans reacted to social, economic, and political conditions between 1820 and 1877, including resistance, rebellions, abolition efforts, court cases, and political involvement.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate social, economic, and political roles of African American men and women during the expansion of the early United States. This may include:
  • Benjamin Banneker, District of Columbia surveyor, planner, astronomer, and naturalist
  • James Derham, first to practice medicine formerly
  • Jupiter Hamon, considered the founder of African-American literature
  • Absalom Jones, early abolitionist and first ordained priest in Episcopal Church
  • Alexander Twilight, first to earn bachelor's degree and first state-elected official in Vermont
description_plain: Your child learns about important African Americans' social, economic, and political roles during the early U.S. expansion, including leaders in science, literature, religion, and government.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate rights and responsibilities of citizens in the United States using the Bill of Rights and various Supreme Court decisions.
  • Free exercise of religion (Kennedy vs. Bremerton School District)
  • Freedom of speech (Brandenburg vs. Ohio)
  • Freedom of press (New York Times vs. United States)
  • Freedom of assembly (Bates vs. Little Rock)
  • Rights of students (Tinker vs. Des Moines)
  • Right against self-incrimination (Miranda vs. Arizona)
  • Right against unreasonable search or seizure (Katz vs. United States)
  • Presumption of innocence (Coffin vs. United States)
  • 14th Amendment (Gitlow vs. New York)
When addressing responsibilities, key topics may include:
  • Obeying the law
  • Respecting rights of others
  • Paying taxes
  • Staying informed
  • Military service
  • Serving as juror
  • Voting
description_plain: Your child can explain citizens’ rights and responsibilities in the U.S., like freedom of speech and religion, obeying laws, paying taxes, voting, and serving on juries.
