subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the sustainability of resources achieved through civic actions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how people’s actions help keep natural resources available for the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Critique various sustainability practices humans use to preserve resources and minimize environmental impact. This may include forest management (e.g., controlled burns), flood control (e.g., river levees, artificial lakes), use of alternative forms of energy (e.g., wind, solar, nuclear), modern agricultural techniques (e.g., organic farming, hydroponics, no-till farming).
description_plain: Your child understands different ways people protect resources and reduce harm to the environment, like controlled forest burns or using solar energy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the impact that resource scarcity has on a region. This may include lack of clean drinking water, land scarcity, labor shortage, and supply chain shortages.
description_plain: Your child can explain how not having enough resources, like clean water or land, affects a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research and evaluate the impact globalization has on states, nations, and regions, including free trade, outsourcing, multinational corporations, and international supply chains.
description_plain: Your child understands how globalization affects countries and regions through things like trade and international companies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze positive and negative consequences of human changes on the physical environment and its effects on other places or regions. This may include forest and land management, mining, flood control, and agriculture.
description_plain: Your child can explain how human activities change the environment and how those changes affect other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine ways people and cultures depend on, adapt to, and interact with the physical environment over time (e.g., technology, habitation, transportation, agriculture, communication).
description_plain: Your child understands how people and cultures depend on and adapt to their environment over time using technology, farming, and transportation.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact humans have on the environment. This includes the distribution, management, and consumption of resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how humans affect the environment by using and managing natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze conflicting territorial claims, including water sources or access, mineral rights, and natural resources.
description_plain: Your child can explain conflicts over land, water, minerals, and other natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain levels of cooperation among people in various places and regions who solve human and environmental issues.
description_plain: Your child understands how people in different places work together to solve problems with people and the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine physical and human characteristics that influence the division and control of the Earth's surface:
  • Resources
  • Land use
  • Ethnicity
  • National identities
  • Natural land barriers (e.g., mountains, rivers, valleys)
  • Military
  • Politics
description_plain: Your child can explain how physical features like mountains and rivers, and human factors like politics and ethnicity, affect how land is divided and controlled.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the social and economic impacts of transportation and communication networks in various regions, including state, regional, and global.
description_plain: Your child understands how transportation and communication affect the economy and society in local, regional, and global areas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare advantages and disadvantages of one location over another for access to factors of production (e.g., human resources, natural resources, capital resources, entrepreneurship).
description_plain: Your child can compare why some places are better than others for things like natural resources, workers, and businesses.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the different types of economic activities supported by natural resources within a region.
  • Arkansas River Valley (e.g., agriculture, coal, natural gas, water)
  • Crowley's Ridge (e.g., agriculture)
  • Mississippi Alluvial Plain (Delta) (e.g., agriculture, waterfowl, fish)
  • Ouachita Mountains (e.g., thermal springs, timber, quartz)
  • Ozark Mountains (e.g., minerals, natural gas)
  • West Gulf Coastal Plain (Timberlands) (e.g., bromine, diamonds, timber, bauxite, oil, wild game)
description_plain: Your child understands the types of jobs and economic activities supported by natural resources in different parts of Arkansas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the characteristics of different physical and cultural regions and how they change over time (through demographic changes, migration, settlement, and conflict). This includes the impact physical geography has on human systems, including politics, culture, economics, and use of resources and how a region or culture interacts with itself, the environment, and other regions and cultures.
description_plain: Your child understands the features of different regions and cultures and how they change over time because of people moving, settling, or conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Research the characteristics of various world regions and cultures:
  • Cultural characteristics (e.g., religious beliefs, celebrations, traditions, language, child-rearing, clothing, food, beliefs, behaviors)
  • Physical characteristics (e.g., mountains, rivers, deserts, plains, bodies of water)
description_plain: Your child can describe the cultural traits like language and traditions, and physical features like mountains and rivers, of different world regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify how natural disasters and other environmental changes have influenced the responses, laws, and/or decisions of political leaders in a region.
description_plain: Your child can explain how natural disasters and environmental changes have affected the decisions and laws made by political leaders in a region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Investigate and draw conclusions about the causes for migration in and out of a region (i.e., push-pull factors) and the impact that migration has on a region.
description_plain: Your child can explore why people move into or out of a region and describe how this movement changes the area.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret demographic data (e.g., population pyramids) to explain variations of populations in different states, places, and regions.
description_plain: Your child can read population data to explain why the number and types of people vary in different states and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the influence of geography on current events and issues and future planning using maps, charts, and available geospatial technologies.
description_plain: Your child can use maps and charts to show how geography affects current events and helps plan for the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine physical and cultural changes regionally over time using primary and secondary sources (e.g., maps, charts, satellite imagery, GIS technologies, beliefs, legends, journals).
description_plain: Your child can study maps, stories, and images to understand how places and cultures have changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze perceptions people have of places and regions around the world, including Arkansas, based on direct experiences (e.g., place of residence, travel) and indirect experiences (e.g., media, books, family, friends) and support or challenge those perceptions using evidence.
description_plain: Your child can think about how people’s experiences shape their ideas about places and use evidence to agree or disagree with those ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare and contrast the combinations of physical and human characteristics that make places and regions similar and different. This may include water systems, topography, natural resources, culture, language, religious beliefs, and population density.
description_plain: Your child can compare what makes places and regions alike or different, like land features, culture, language, and population.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct visual and written explanations of the spatial organization and spatial patterns of people, places, and environments. This may include maps, charts, and graphs.
description_plain: Your child can create maps, charts, or written explanations to show how people, places, and environments are arranged.

subject: Social Studies
description: Research how environmental characteristics have impacted the culture of states, places, and regions over time. This may include seafaring/trading in coastal communities, farming cultures in river valleys, and nomadic cultures in arid climates.
description_plain: Your child can research how the environment has influenced the culture of different places, like farming near rivers or trading by the sea.

subject: Social Studies
description: Collect geographic data to explain a pattern or phenomenon or to ask or answer a geographic question (e.g., surveys, population density, physical boundaries)
description_plain: Your child can gather geographic information to explain patterns or answer questions about a place.

subject: Social Studies
description: Critique the characteristics and functions of various maps, graphs, and other geographic tools to understand spatial information about different states, places, and regions (e.g., traditional maps, diagrams, aerial photographs, remotely sensed images, geographic visualization, global positioning systems, geographic information systems).
description_plain: Your child can review different maps and tools to understand how they show information about places and regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate proper etiquette for interacting with the Arkansas and American flags and evaluate the significance of a flag in symbolizing nationalities and boundaries around the world.
description_plain: Your child can show respect when interacting with the Arkansas and American flags and explain what flags represent for countries and borders.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to interpret spatial information. This includes the spatial organization of people, cultures, places, and environments within various regions, and geographic skills to interpret the past, the present, and plan for the future.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and charts to learn about where people and places are and how to use these tools to understand history and plan ahead.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the impact of exploration on civilizations in the Americas:
  • Aztec
  • Maya
  • Inca
  • Pre-Columbian societies in Arkansas such as Mississippians, Plum Bayou Culture, and prehistoric Caddo
description_plain: Your child can describe how exploration affected civilizations like the Aztec, Maya, Inca, and early groups in Arkansas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the impact of disease and war on life within Europe including the Crusades, Feudalism, and Black Death.
description_plain: Your child can explain how disease and war changed life in Europe during events like the Crusades and the Black Death.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze effects of cultural interactions and connections on civilizations over time. This may include art, literature, religion, architecture, Zheng He's warships, printing press, Maya calendar, Great Zimbabwe, and technological innovations.
description_plain: Your child can study how cultures influenced each other over time through art, religion, technology, and other connections.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine the effects invaders brought to various societies, including disease, famine, and cultural assimilation. Groups may include:
  • Mongols
  • Vikings
  • Crusaders
  • Maya
  • Aztecs
description_plain: Your child can look at how invaders like the Mongols and Vikings brought changes such as disease and new cultures to societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the global influence and impact of the achievements and perspectives of various individuals. This may include Gutenberg, Joan of Arc, Niccolo Machiavelli, Saladin, Sundiata Keita, Mansa Musa, and Genghis Khan.
description_plain: Your child can explain how important people like Gutenberg and Joan of Arc influenced the world.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine key concepts and influences of major belief systems on societies:
  • Buddhism
  • Christianity
  • Confucianism
  • Hinduism
  • Judaism
  • Islam
description_plain: Your child can learn about major religions and how they shaped societies, including Buddhism, Christianity, and Islam.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate how the fall of the Roman Empire affected Europe:
  • Loss of a centralized power (i.e. power vacuum)
  • Rise of power of the Roman Catholic Church
  • Norse invasions
  • Frankish Kingdoms (Charlemagne)
description_plain: Your child can explain how the fall of the Roman Empire changed Europe, including the rise of the Church and invasions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine causes and effects of conflict within and among the major empires and civilizations. This may include the fall of the Roman Empire, Byzantine Empire, Mongol Empire, Songhai Empire, and Maya civilization.
description_plain: Your child can study causes and effects of conflicts in major empires like Rome and the Mongols.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the contributions of major empires and civilizations of the world. This may include the decimal, art, literature, architecture, law, fables, and proverbs (e.g., Aesop, Confucius, and Ancient Hebrews) of major world civilizations:
  • African kingdoms and empires
  • Middle Eastern and Muslim Empires
  • European kingdoms and empires
  • East Asian empires and dynasties
description_plain: Your child can describe important contributions like art and laws from major world empires in Africa, the Middle East, Europe, and Asia.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine key concepts and influences of major belief systems on societies:
  • Buddhism
  • Christianity
  • Confucianism
  • Hinduism
  • Judaism
description_plain: Your child can learn about key beliefs of major religions and how they influenced societies.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine causes and effects of conflict within and among the major empires (e.g., Persian War, Punic Wars, Greek city-states).
description_plain: Your child can explain causes and results of wars between empires like the Greeks and Romans.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance and lasting effects of contributions made by major empires and civilizations of the world such as the concept of zero, crossbow, architecture, government, and calendar.
description_plain: Your child can explain why inventions and ideas from ancient empires, like the calendar and government, still matter today.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Compare reasons for the rise and decline of major empires and civilizations:
  • Egypt
  • Babylonia
  • Kingdom of Kush
  • Mali Empire
  • Greece
  • Gupta
  • China (Han)
  • Rome
  • Mayan Empire
description_plain: Your child can compare why great empires like Egypt and Rome grew strong and why they eventually declined.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify social and cultural effects of militarization and the emergence of new kingdoms on early civilizations (e.g., chariot transport, warfare, invaders).
description_plain: Your child can identify how war and new kingdoms affected early civilizations, including changes in daily life and culture.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Compare characteristics, contributions, and achievements of early river valley civilizations:
  • Mesopotamia
  • Egypt
  • Indus River Valley
  • China
description_plain: Your child can compare early river valley civilizations like Mesopotamia and Egypt and what they achieved.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Evaluate the characteristics necessary for a civilization:
  • Government/leadership structure
  • Culture (e.g., religion/beliefs, accepted practices/traditions)
  • Specialization of labor
  • Mastery of food supply
  • Technology
  • Economic systems
  • Education
  • Communication systems (e.g., spoken language, alphabetic and/or numeric systems, hieroglyphics, illustrations)
description_plain: Your child can explain what a civilization needs, like government, culture, jobs, food, technology, and communication.

subject: Social Studies
description: Construct arguments about lasting achievements of early civilizations.
description_plain: Your child can make arguments about important achievements from early civilizations that still affect us today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the characteristics of hunter-gatherer and agrarian societies. This may include tools, shelter, diet, use of fire, cave paintings, artifacts, clothing, rituals, daily life, gender roles.
description_plain: Your child can compare how hunter-gatherers and farming societies lived, including their tools, homes, and daily activities.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the beginning of civilization (World Era 1) through 1500 C.E. (World Era 5). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of nations and cultures of the world.
description_plain: Your child understands major historical periods from the start of civilization to 1500 C.E., including how societies changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare ways spatial patterns of economic activities in a place change over time because of interactions with nearby and distant places.
description_plain: Your child can compare how economic activities in a place change over time because of connections with other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the impact of major population shifts in various eras and regions.
description_plain: Your child can explain how big population changes affected different times and places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Determine how environmental and cultural characteristics affected the distribution and movement of people, goods, and ideas in various regions of the world.
description_plain: Your child can figure out how the environment and culture influenced where people, goods, and ideas moved around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze relationships between human settlements and movements and the location and use of natural resources in various regions.
description_plain: Your child can analyze how people’s settlements and movements relate to natural resources in different regions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways climate and environmental characteristics influenced where groups lived and how the groups adjusted to the environment in various civilizations.
description_plain: Your child can explain how climate and environment affected where groups lived and how they adapted.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways cultural characteristics influenced population distribution in various civilizations. This may include Greek city-states, Phoenecian colonies and trade cities, and Chinese agrarian society.
description_plain: Your child can analyze how culture influenced where people lived in civilizations like Greece and China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze effects of human-generated changes on the physical environment in various places and regions over time such as large building projects, canals, irrigation, farming, and deforestation.
description_plain: Your child can study how human activities like farming and building changed the environment over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Synthesize information from a variety of sources to construct maps and other geographic representations to ask and answer compelling questions.
description_plain: Your child can use information from different sources to create maps and answer important questions about places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain relationships between physical and human characteristics in various places using a variety of geographic tools such as maps, charts, and graphs.
description_plain: Your child can use maps, charts, and graphs to explain how physical features and human activities are connected in different places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the locations and environmental and cultural characteristics of various societies using maps, globes, and other geographic tools.
description_plain: Your child will use maps and globes to study where different societies are and learn about their environments and cultures.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to understand, analyze, and explain human interaction with each other and with the environment. This includes the spatial characteristics and patterns of human settlement and connections between global regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how maps and other tools help us learn about where people live and how they interact with the environment and each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify barriers to trade and their impact on civilizations such as war/conflict, infrastructure, lack of technology, and geographic limitations.
description_plain: Your child understands what stops trade, like wars or lack of technology, and how these problems affect civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the economic impact of war and conflict on societies and civilizations such as siege, reduction of labor, and spread of disease.
description_plain: Your child can explain how war and conflict hurt societies by causing things like fewer workers and spreading disease.

subject: Social Studies
description: Sequence the development of currency as a medium of exchange (i.e., barter > trade > progression of currency).
description_plain: Your child will put in order how money developed from bartering to using different kinds of currency.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the emergence of new economic systems and their impact on civilizations (e.g., manorialism, mercantilism).
description_plain: Your child can explain how new economic systems, like manorialism and mercantilism, changed civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare effects of supply and demand on early markets.
description_plain: Your child will compare how supply and demand affected early markets.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze ways human, natural, and capital resources were organized to produce and deliver goods and services in early civilizations (e.g., caravans, public works projects, Silk Road, trade routes).
description_plain: Your child can describe how people used natural, human, and capital resources to make and deliver goods in early civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate how the division of labor and specialization led to the development of civilizations. This may include the increased productivity and quality of life civilizations experienced as they transitioned out of hunter-gatherer clusters and into agrarian, pastoral, and even feudal societies in which highly specialized skills could be developed, including artisans, cultivators, educators, and warriors.
description_plain: Your child understands how dividing work and specializing in skills helped civilizations grow and improve life.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze historical developments in various regions across the world using models of economic decision-making. Historical developments may include nomadic vs. agrarian, invasions, bartering, and trade.
description_plain: Your child can study historical events like farming, invasions, and trade using economic decision-making ideas.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine ways trade-offs have allowed civilizations to get the most out of scarce resources.
description_plain: Your child will explain how civilizations made choices to use limited resources in the best way.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes the exchange of goods and services; role of producers, consumers, and government in the marketplace; and growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how people make economic choices, how goods and services are exchanged, and the roles of producers, consumers, and government in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the development of policies to address public problems in various civilizations over time.
description_plain: Your child can explain how different civilizations created rules to solve public problems over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the impact of rules and laws on society and reasons people change rules and laws over time.
description_plain: Your child will analyze how laws affect society and why people change laws as time passes.

subject: Social Studies
description: Evaluate the function and effects of rules, laws and treaties on civilizations by using historical documents. This may include the Code of Hammurabi, the Torah, the Twelve Tables, Law of Manu, Ancient Mayan law, Tang Code, and Justinian Code.
description_plain: Your child can use old documents to understand how rules and laws worked in ancient civilizations.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare rights, responsibilities, and privileges of individuals in various societies to 1500 C.E.
description_plain: Your child will compare what rights and responsibilities people had in different societies before 1500 C.E.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate proper etiquette for interacting with the Arkansas and American flags while analyzing the role of flags and symbols in world history.
description_plain: Your child understands how to show respect for the Arkansas and American flags and learns why flags and symbols are important in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare ways in which various civilizations foster social responsibility and civic virtues.
description_plain: Your child can compare how different civilizations encourage people to be responsible and good citizens.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the structure of leadership/government and functions of civilizations in different times and places (e.g., patriarchal, matriarchal, tribal, city-state, national).
description_plain: Your child will compare different types of governments and leadership styles from various times and places.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Research origins, purposes, and structures of government citing primary and secondary sources:
  • Origins (e.g., Code of Hammurabi, Jewish Torah, Mandate of Heaven, Justinian Code, Twelve Tables, Magna Carta)
  • Purposes (e.g., belief systems, resource allocation)
  • Structures (e.g., patriarchal, matriarchal, tribal, city-state, national)
description_plain: Your child can research where governments started, why they exist, and how they are organized using different sources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of origins, structures, and functions of institutions and laws on society and citizens. This includes personal civic rights, roles, responsibilities, and processes by which laws are made and amended.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and institutions affect people’s rights and responsibilities and how laws are made and changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply constitutional principles, such as popular sovereignty and checks and balances, to a variety of current issues using landmark rulings.
description_plain: Your child can apply ideas from the Constitution, like people's power and checks and balances, to current issues using important court decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Apply constitutional principles, such as popular sovereignty and checks and balances, to a variety of current issues using landmark rulings.
description_plain: Your child can apply ideas from the Constitution, like people's power and checks and balances, to current issues using important court decisions.

description: Examine causes and effects of amendments to the U.S. Constitution after the Bill of Rights.
description_plain: Your child will study why and how the U.S. Constitution was changed after the Bill of Rights.

description: Analyze various federal laws, policies, and rulings concerning civil rights of individuals and groups.
description_plain: Your child can analyze laws and court decisions about civil rights for individuals and groups.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Define different judicial philosophies and evaluate how they have governed Supreme Court decisions over time:
  • Judicial activism
  • Judicial restraint
  • Originalism
  • Textualism
  • Contextualism
  • Judicial pragmatism (i.e., "Living Constitution")
description_plain: Your child will learn about different ways judges think and how these ideas affect Supreme Court decisions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways the U.S. Constitution and U.S. Supreme Court decisions have defined, recognized, and protected or not protected civil liberties, due process, and equal protection from governmental intrusion over time.
description_plain: Your child can explain how the Constitution and Supreme Court have protected or not protected people's freedoms and rights over time.

description: Analyze major U.S. Supreme Court decisions affecting our understanding of the U.S. Constitution, including Marbury vs. Madison and McCullah vs. Maryland.
description_plain: Your child will study important Supreme Court cases that shaped how we understand the Constitution.

description_plain: Your child will study why American colonists decided to break away from British rule because of laws and actions that affected their lives.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the influence of social, economic, religious, and political factors within the American colonies, including the concept of self-government, on the formation of the American government.
description_plain: Your child can analyze how ideas like self-government influenced the American colonies to form their own government.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine how the writings of John Locke, John Winthrop, Charles-Louis Montesquieu, Jean-Jacques Rousseau, William Blackstone, and other sources influenced the formation of the United States government. Other sources may include:
  • Key documents (e.g., Magna Carta, English Bill of Rights, Mayflower Compact)
  • Greek (e.g., Aristotle, Greek democracy)
  • Roman (e.g., separation of powers, representative democracy)
  • Judeo-Christian traditions
description_plain: Your child will examine how writings and ideas from important thinkers and documents influenced the U.S. government.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze the effectiveness of citizens, institutions, and the government in addressing environmental and social issues at the local, state, and national levels since 1968:
  • Environmental issues: [e.g., creation of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), Earth Day movement, National Environmental Policy Act of 1970, push towards alternative forms of energy]
  • Social issues: (e.g., increased government involvement in housing and healthcare, expansion of civil rights, Reagan Revolution, welfare and unemployment reform, AIDS crisis, coronavirus pandemic)
description_plain: Your child can explain how people, organizations, and the government have worked to solve environmental and social problems since 1968.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine the social and economic effects of globalization on the United States:
  • Trade agreements and organizations [e.g., European Union, North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA), World Trade Organization (WTO)]
  • Economic shifts due to international agreements (e.g., cheap labor, decline in American manufacturing, multinational corporations)
  • Increased immigration from Latin America, South Asia, and East Asia
description_plain: Your child understands how global trade, economic changes, and immigration have affected the United States.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Construct explanations of change and continuity in foreign policy since 2001 after the September 11th, terrorist attacks in New York City and Washington, D.C.:
  • Response of the Bush administration
  • Wars in Afghanistan and Iraq
  • Domestic and international efforts to combat terrorism (e.g., the Patriot Act, Hamdi vs. Rumsfeld)
description_plain: Your child can explain how U.S. foreign policy changed after 2001, including responses to terrorism and wars in Afghanistan and Iraq.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Examine reasons for and consequences of United States involvement in foreign conflicts, including American foreign policy under multiple administrations, and the changing view of America's international role:
  • Foreign policies of Bill Clinton, George W. Bush, Barack Obama, Donald Trump, and Joe Biden
  • Role of NATO in Eastern Europe and post-Communist Russia
  • Response to China as a competitor and possible threat
  • U.S. involved conflicts: (e.g., Grenada, First and Second Gulf Wars, Iran-Contra, Nicaragua, Libya, Syria, Israel, Afghanistan)
description_plain: Your child can describe why the U.S. got involved in various foreign conflicts and how America’s role in the world has changed under different presidents.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze effects of domestic policies on Americans in marginalized groups:
  • Growth of African American middle class, increased political representation
  • Protection of rights for Americans with disabilities (e.g., Individuals with Disabilities Education Act, Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990)
  • Increased recognition of Indigenous sovereignty (e.g., Indian Child Welfare Act, Tribal Self Governance Act)
  • Progress of women's rights (e.g., Equal Pay Act of 1963, Title IX)
  • Other impactful policies (e.g., "War on Drugs," welfare state, Patriot Act)
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and policies have affected African Americans, people with disabilities, Indigenous peoples, women, and others.

Analyze the expanded role of the federal government's domestic interventions and fiscal policy in response to the Great Depression:
  • New Deal legislation (e.g., Emergency Banking Relief Act, Agricultural Adjustment Act, Social Security Act, National Labor Relations Act)
  • Federal work relief agencies (e.g., Tennessee Valley Authority, Civilian Conservation Corps, Public Works Administration, Work Progress Administration)
  • Changing role of the federal government in the economy (e.g., social security, minimum wage)
description_plain: Your child understands how the federal government expanded its role during the Great Depression through laws and work programs.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Analyze national and international causes of the Great Depression:
  • Political causes: (e.g., protectionism, tariffs)
  • Economic and monetary causes: (e.g., speculation, overproduction, farm surplus, foreclosures, easy credit, deflation, unemployment)
  • Environmental causes (e.g., Dust Bowl)
description_plain: Your child can explain the political, economic, and environmental causes of the Great Depression.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Understand the different components of loans by:
  • Differentiating between the types of loans (e.g., payday, auto, home, personal, student)
  • Examining the lending process from application to approval
  • Calculating true costs associated with loans (e.g., term length, interest rate)
  • Understanding the factors that contribute to different interest rates
  • Evaluating the implications of obtaining and/or defaulting on a loan
description_plain: Your child can explain different types of loans, how loans are approved, figure out the real costs of loans, understand why interest rates vary, and think about what happens if a loan is not paid back.

description:
Understand different components of credit by:
  • Comparing and contrasting sources of credit (e.g., car loans, student loans, credit cards)
  • Discussing the establishment and use of credit
  • Identifying the factors that contribute to a credit score
  • Calculating the actual costs associated with credit
  • Discussing methods of solving credit problems
  • Evaluating the risks associated with overextending credit
description_plain: Your child can compare different types of credit, explain how credit is used, know what affects credit scores, calculate credit costs, find ways to fix credit problems, and understand the risks of borrowing too much.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the Emergence of the First Global Age, 1450-1770 (World Era 6), to the Twentieth Century Since 1945 (World Era 9). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of nations and cultures of the world.
description_plain: Your child understands important world history periods from 1450 to today, including how societies and cultures have changed and how people view history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the Great Depression and World War II, 1929-1945 (Era 8), to the Contemporary United States, 1968 to Present (Era 10). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States
description_plain: Your child understands important events and changes in the U.S. from the Great Depression and World War II to today, including how people see and tell history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from Civil War and Reconstruction (Era 5) to the Emergence of Modern America, 1890-1930 (Era 7). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands key events and changes in the U.S. from the Civil War to the early 1900s, including how people view and explain history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the United States' Beginnings (Era 1) through 1850 (Era 4). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands important U.S. history from the country’s beginnings through 1850, including how society, economy, and politics changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the beginning of civilization (World Era 1) through 1500 C.E. (World Era 5). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of nations and cultures of the world.
description_plain: Your child understands major historical periods from the start of civilization to 1500 C.E., including how societies changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the differences in sources of information from the present and the past (e.g., telegraph, pony express, newspaper, telephone, TV, Internet).
description_plain: Your child can compare how people get information now and in the past using things like telegraphs, newspapers, phones, TV, and the Internet.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe materials and methods that allow people to learn about the past (e.g., photos, artifacts, diaries, oral history, stories).
description_plain: Your child can name things like photos, artifacts, diaries, and stories that help us learn about the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the different points of view represented in a single historical event.
description_plain: Your child can recognize that different people may see the same event in different ways.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Identify the purpose of national holidays and describe the people or events celebrated.
  • Independence Day
  • Thanksgiving
  • Memorial Day
  • Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr. Day
  • President's Day
  • Veteran's Day
description_plain: Your child can tell why we have national holidays and who or what we celebrate on days like Independence Day and Martin Luther King Jr. Day.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize historic figures and other people who have made an impact on history.
description_plain: Your child can recognize important people who have made a difference in history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare the life of a student today (present) to the life of a student in the past using visual representations (e.g., growing food, rules and laws, making clothing, transportation, communication).
description_plain: Your child can compare what life is like for students today and in the past using pictures about things like food, rules, clothes, travel, and talking.

subject: Social Studies
description: Develop a timeline to sequence significant events in students' lives.
description_plain: Your child can make a timeline to put important events from their own life in order.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Discuss a sequence of events using chronological terms such as first, next, last, before, after. Sequence of events may include:
  • Daily classroom activities
  • Significant events in students' lives
  • Typical day in the life of the student
description_plain: Your child can talk about the order of events using words like first, next, last, before, and after for things like their day or important life events.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand chronology, patterns of continuity, and change over time. This includes the contextualization of historical events and ways people gather, view, construct, and interpret historical evidence.
description_plain: Your child understands how history is organized over time and how people study and interpret historical evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact Indigenous peoples have on the environment. This includes the communal view of how Indigenous peoples utilized the land and resources; and the characteristics of the original Indigenous peoples of Arkansas by analyzing artifacts, artwork, and other sources.
description_plain: Your child understands how Indigenous peoples cared for the land and resources and can learn about Arkansas Indigenous peoples by looking at artifacts and art.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact humans have on the environment. This includes the distribution, management, and consumption of resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how humans affect the environment by using and managing natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the characteristics of different physical and cultural regions and how they change over time (through demographic changes, migration, settlement, and conflict). This includes the impact physical geography has on human systems, including politics, culture, economics, and use of resources and how a region or culture interacts with itself, the environment, and other regions and cultures.
description_plain: Your child understands the features of different regions and cultures and how they change over time because of people moving, settling, or conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to interpret spatial information. This includes spatial organization of people, cultures, places, and environments within various regions and geographic skills to interpret the past, present, and plan for the future.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and charts to learn about where people and places are and how to use these tools to understand history and plan ahead.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the needs of people during natural and human-made disasters.
description_plain: Your child can talk about what people need during natural disasters or accidents caused by people.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss products and traditions that connect people around the world (e.g., where products are made, celebrations, dance, art, food, toys).
description_plain: Your child can talk about products and traditions like food, art, and celebrations that connect people around the world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify people, goods, and ideas that move from place to place.
description_plain: Your child can identify people, things, and ideas that move from one place to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify natural resources that meet the needs of a community such as timber, minerals, oil, coal, and natural gas.
description_plain: Your child can name natural resources like trees, minerals, oil, coal, and gas that help meet community needs.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the influence of weather and climate on people's daily lives.
description_plain: Your child can explain how weather and climate affect what people do every day.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss cultural characteristics among families and in the community such as art, celebrations, food, language, music, and traditions.
description_plain: Your child can talk about family and community traditions like art, celebrations, food, language, and music.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe ways humans have impacted the environment
  • planting trees
  • reducing waste
  • littering
  • polluting
description_plain: Your child can describe ways people help or hurt the environment, like planting trees or polluting.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create maps of familiar places such as the school, playground, or neighborhood.
description_plain: Your child can draw maps of places they know well, like their school or neighborhood.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and describe the physical characteristics of a place such as rivers, mountains, and forests using maps, globes, and photographs.
description_plain: Your child can use maps, globes, and photos to describe features like rivers, mountains, and forests.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe familiar places using words that communicate location (e.g., beside, past, before), direction (e.g., right/left), and distance (e.g., long/short).
description_plain: Your child can describe places using words about location, direction, and distance, like beside, left, or far.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to understand, analyze, and explain human interaction with each other and with the environment. This includes the spatial characteristics and patterns of human settlement and connections between global regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how maps and other tools help us learn about where people live and how they interact with the environment and each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the factors affecting income, wealth, and financial risk. This includes the role of credit in personal finance.
description_plain: Your child understands what affects income, wealth, and financial risks, including how credit works in personal finance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy. This includes ways in which trade leads to increased economic interdependence.
description_plain: Your child understands how economies grow, stay stable, and depend on each other globally, including how trade increases connections between countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the growth, stability, and interdependence within a national economy. This includes the current and future state of the economy using economic indicators and monetary and fiscal policies for a variety of economic conditions.
description_plain: Your child understands how a country's economy grows and stays stable, and how different factors like government policies affect it now and in the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the exchange of goods and services. This includes different allocation methods and changes in supply and demand; the role of producers, consumers, and government in a market economy; and the degree of competition among buyers and sellers in markets.
description_plain: Your child understands how goods and services are exchanged, how supply and demand change, and the roles of producers, consumers, government, and competition in markets.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes considering the marginal costs and benefits of alternatives.
description_plain: Your child understands how making economic decisions involves thinking about the extra costs and benefits of different options.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify where products used in daily life are produced.
description_plain: Your child can identify where everyday products are made.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify common products that come from other countries.
description_plain: Your child can name common products that come from other countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss examples of goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can talk about examples of goods (things) and services (help) people use.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the reasons for and places where people save money (e.g., piggy banks, wallets, banks).
description_plain: Your child can explain why and where people save money, like in piggy banks or banks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize that consumers use money as a medium of exchange to satisfy economic wants and needs.
description_plain: Your child understands that people use money to buy things they want or need.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways people buy and sell goods (i.e., markets).
description_plain: Your child can identify ways people buy and sell things, like at markets.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss ways human, natural, and capital resources are used in the production of goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how people use workers, natural materials, and tools to make goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways people create goods and services.
description_plain: Your child can identify ways people make goods and provide services.

subject: Social Studies
description: State the reasons behind making a personal decision.
description_plain: Your child can explain why they make certain personal choices.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss needs and wants and how they are restricted by limited resources.
description_plain: Your child can talk about needs and wants and understand that we can't always have everything because resources are limited.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes the exchange of goods and services; role of producers, consumers, and government in the marketplace; and growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how people make economic choices, how goods and services are exchanged, and the roles of producers, consumers, and government in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the structure and functions of various types of tribal government and how they exercise their powers. This includes the progress and challenges of present-day Native America.
description_plain: Your child understands how different tribal governments work and how Native American communities face challenges and make progress today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of origins, structures, and functions of institutions and laws on society and Indigenous peoples. This includes the relationship of the Native nations and the United States during various time periods.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and institutions affect society and Native peoples, including how Native nations and the United States have related over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the process of making and changing laws and the ways institutions work together in carrying out the laws.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws are made and changed and how different institutions work together to enforce them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the role of citizens in society, the ways the government protects the rights of citizens, the electoral process, and the role of political parties.
description_plain: Your child understands what citizens do in society, how the government protects their rights, how elections work, and what political parties do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the structure and functions of various types of government and how they exercise their powers.
description_plain: Your child understands different types of governments and how they use their powers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the importance of problem solving related to classroom issues.
description_plain: Your child can talk about how to solve problems that happen in the classroom.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Discuss ways people improve communities which may include:
  • being a good neighbor
  • volunteering/helping
  • recycling
  • donating personal items/toys
description_plain: Your child understands ways people help improve their communities, like being a good neighbor, volunteering, recycling, or donating items.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the need for rules.
description_plain: Discuss the need for rules.

subject: Social Studies
description: Follow agreed-upon rules for listening and having a discussion in the classroom.
description_plain: Your child follows classroom rules for listening and taking part in discussions.

subject: Social Studies
description: Demonstrate responsibilities of being a good citizen at school.
description_plain: Your child shows how to be a responsible and good citizen at school.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Recognize state and national symbols and patriotic songs:
  • American flag
  • Star Spangled Banner
  • Recitation of Pledge of Allegiance
  • Arkansas flag
  • Apple Blossom
  • Honey Bee
  • Mockingbird
  • Pine Tree
description_plain: Your child recognizes important state and national symbols and songs, like the American flag, the Pledge of Allegiance, and the Arkansas state symbols.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the characteristics and responsibilities of a leader.
description_plain: Your child can identify what makes a good leader and what leaders are responsible for.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the purpose of classroom or school rules in establishing communities and ways of living and working together.
description_plain: Your child understands why classroom and school rules help everyone live and work together.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of origins, structures, and functions of institutions and laws on society and citizens. This includes personal civic rights, roles, responsibilities, and processes by which laws are made and amended.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and institutions affect people’s rights and responsibilities and how laws are made and changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the Emergence of the First Global Age, 1450-1770 (World Era 6), to the Twentieth Century Since 1945 (World Era 9). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of nations and cultures of the world.
description_plain: Your child understands important world history periods from 1450 to today, including how societies and cultures have changed and how people view history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the Great Depression and World War II, 1929-1945 (Era 8), to the Contemporary United States, 1968 to Present (Era 10). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States
description_plain: Your child understands important events and changes in the U.S. from the Great Depression and World War II to today, including how people see and tell history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from Civil War and Reconstruction (Era 5) to the Emergence of Modern America, 1890-1930 (Era 7). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands key events and changes in the U.S. from the Civil War to the early 1900s, including how people view and explain history.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the United States' Beginnings (Era 1) through 1850 (Era 4). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of the United States.
description_plain: Your child understands important U.S. history from the country’s beginnings through 1850, including how society, economy, and politics changed.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand key historical periods from the beginning of civilization (World Era 1) through 1500 C.E. (World Era 5). This includes the patterns of social, economic, and political change over time and the ways people view, construct, and interpret the history of nations and cultures of the world.
description_plain: Your child understands major historical periods from the start of civilization to 1500 C.E., including how societies changed over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways that a historian can determine the time, place, and credibility of a source (e.g., publication date, author, place of origin, accuracy of facts).
description_plain: Your child can find clues like dates and authors to decide when and where a historical source was made and if it is trustworthy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Draw conclusions about life in the past using historical records and artifacts (e.g., photos, diaries, oral history).
description_plain: Your child uses old photos, diaries, and stories to learn about what life was like in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss different accounts of the same historical event.
description_plain: Your child talks about different versions of the same historical event.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of national holidays and the achievement of people associated with them.
description_plain: Your child explains why national holidays are important and who is honored on those days.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Retell stories of historical events, American legends, and people who played a role in history. This may include:
  • Pocahontas
  • Sacagawea
  • Lewis and Clark
  • Hernando de Soto
  • Henri de Tonti
  • Paul Revere
  • Benjamin Franklin
  • Booker T. Washington
  • Frederick Douglass
description_plain: Your child retells stories about important historical events and people like Pocahontas, Paul Revere, and Frederick Douglass.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare present day families, objects, and events with those in the past using visual representations, news stories, and artifacts (e.g., daily life tasks, food, clothing, transportation, communication, recreation).
description_plain: Your child compares families, objects, and events from today with those from the past using pictures and stories.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create timelines to sequence events from different times using chronological terms.
description_plain: Your child makes timelines to put events in the order they happened using words like first, next, and last.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Explain similarities and differences of everyday life in different times using chronological terms. This may include:
  • Daily tasks
  • Food
  • Clothing
  • Transportation
  • Communication
  • Recreation
  • Celebrations
description_plain: Your child explains how daily life, food, clothes, transportation, communication, fun, and celebrations are similar or different now and in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand chronology, patterns of continuity, and change over time. This includes the contextualization of historical events and ways people gather, view, construct, and interpret historical evidence.
description_plain: Your child understands how history is organized over time and how people study and interpret historical evidence.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact Indigenous peoples have on the environment. This includes the communal view of how Indigenous peoples utilized the land and resources; and the characteristics of the original Indigenous peoples of Arkansas by analyzing artifacts, artwork, and other sources.
description_plain: Your child understands how Indigenous peoples cared for the land and resources and can learn about Arkansas Indigenous peoples by looking at artifacts and art.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact humans have on the environment. This includes the distribution, management, and consumption of resources.
description_plain: Your child understands how humans affect the environment by using and managing natural resources.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the characteristics of different physical and cultural regions and how they change over time (through demographic changes, migration, settlement, and conflict). This includes the impact physical geography has on human systems, including politics, culture, economics, and use of resources and how a region or culture interacts with itself, the environment, and other regions and cultures.
description_plain: Your child understands the features of different regions and cultures and how they change over time because of people moving, settling, or conflicts.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to interpret spatial information. This includes spatial organization of people, cultures, places, and environments within various regions and geographic skills to interpret the past, present, and plan for the future.
description_plain: Your child understands how to use maps, globes, and charts to learn about where people and places are and how to use these tools to understand history and plan ahead.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify ways to help people who are going through natural and human-made disasters.
description_plain: Your child identifies ways to help people affected by natural disasters or accidents.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how the products that are consumed connect the local community to other parts of the country and rest of the world such as clothes, toys, and food.
description_plain: Your child talks about how things we use like clothes, toys, and food connect our community to other places in the country and world.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss reasons and methods people, goods, and ideas move from place to place.
description_plain: Your child discusses why and how people, goods, and ideas move from one place to another.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain ways people utilize natural resources such as timber, minerals, oil, coal, and natural gas in their community.
description_plain: Your child explains how people use natural resources like wood, minerals, oil, coal, and gas in their community.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the influence of weather, climate, and physical characteristics on people's daily lives.
description_plain: Your child describes how weather, climate, and land features affect how people live every day.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss how cultural characteristics contribute to diversity in a community, place, or region.
description_plain: Your child talks about how different cultures add to the variety of people in a community or region.

subject: Social Studies
description: Interpret effects of human impact on the environment.
description_plain: Your child understands how people’s actions can change the environment.

subject: Social Studies
description: Create and label maps of local areas using titles, symbols, legends, and a compass rose.
description_plain: Your child can make and label maps of local places using titles, symbols, legends, and a compass rose.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the physical and human characteristics of a place including roads, buildings, and borders using maps, globes, and photographs.
description_plain: Your child describes places by looking at roads, buildings, and borders using maps, globes, and photos.

subject: Social Studies
description: Show relationships between familiar places using map keys, legends, compass rose, and directional words.
description_plain: Your child shows how familiar places are connected using map keys, legends, compass rose, and directions like north and south.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the purpose of geographic tools (e.g., maps, globes, charts, graphs) to understand, analyze, and explain human interaction with each other and with the environment. This includes the spatial characteristics and patterns of human settlement and connections between global regions.
description_plain: Your child understands how maps and other tools help us learn about where people live and how they interact with the environment and each other.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the factors affecting income, wealth, and financial risk. This includes the role of credit in personal finance.
description_plain: Your child understands what affects income, wealth, and financial risks, including how credit works in personal finance.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy. This includes ways in which trade leads to increased economic interdependence.
description_plain: Your child understands how economies grow, stay stable, and depend on each other globally, including how trade increases connections between countries.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the growth, stability, and interdependence within a national economy. This includes the current and future state of the economy using economic indicators and monetary and fiscal policies for a variety of economic conditions.
description_plain: Your child understands how a country's economy grows and stays stable, and how different factors like government policies affect it now and in the future.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the exchange of goods and services. This includes different allocation methods and changes in supply and demand; the role of producers, consumers, and government in a market economy; and the degree of competition among buyers and sellers in markets.
description_plain: Your child understands how goods and services are exchanged, how supply and demand change, and the roles of producers, consumers, government, and competition in markets.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes considering the marginal costs and benefits of alternatives.
description_plain: Your child understands how making economic decisions involves thinking about the extra costs and benefits of different options.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify goods that are imported to and exported from Arkansas and the United States.
description_plain: Your child identifies goods that Arkansas and the United States send to and receive from other places.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify reasons why people trade goods and services between countries.
description_plain: Your child understands why people trade goods and services between countries.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Identify examples of public goods and services that governments provide
  • police officers
  • roads
  • traffic lights
  • state parks
  • clean air/water
  • public education
description_plain: Your child knows about public goods and services the government provides, like police, roads, parks, and schools.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the reasons why and the tools and techniques people use to save money.
description_plain: Your child talks about why people save money and the tools they use to do it.

subject: Social Studies
description: Classify exchanges consumers make as monetary or bartering.
description_plain: Your child can tell the difference between buying with money and trading goods without money.

subject: Social Studies
description: Recognize that markets exist when buyers and sellers exchange goods and services.
description_plain: Your child understands that markets happen when buyers and sellers exchange goods and services.

subject: Social Studies
description: Compare ways human, natural, and capital resources are used in the production of goods and services now and long ago.
description_plain: Your child compares how people use human, natural, and capital resources to make goods and services now and in the past.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the importance of human capital (i.e., knowledge, skills, education, experience) and the relationship between work and income.
description_plain: Your child talks about how knowledge, skills, and education help people work and earn money.

subject: Social Studies
description: List costs and benefits of making a decision.
description_plain: Your child lists the good and bad things about making a choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify examples of scarcity and opportunity cost.
description_plain: Your child identifies times when there is not enough of something and what is given up when making a choice.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of economic decision-making. This includes the exchange of goods and services; role of producers, consumers, and government in the marketplace; and growth, stability, and interdependence within a global economy.
description_plain: Your child understands how people make economic choices, how goods and services are exchanged, and the roles of producers, consumers, and government in the economy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the structure and functions of various types of tribal government and how they exercise their powers. This includes the progress and challenges of present-day Native America.
description_plain: Your child understands how different tribal governments work and how Native American communities face challenges and make progress today.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of origins, structures, and functions of institutions and laws on society and Indigenous peoples. This includes the relationship of the Native nations and the United States during various time periods.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and institutions affect society and Native peoples, including how Native nations and the United States have related over time.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the process of making and changing laws and the ways institutions work together in carrying out the laws.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws are made and changed and how different institutions work together to enforce them.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the role of citizens in society, the ways the government protects the rights of citizens, the electoral process, and the role of political parties.
description_plain: Your child understands what citizens do in society, how the government protects their rights, how elections work, and what political parties do.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the structure and functions of various types of government and how they exercise their powers.
description_plain: Your child understands different types of governments and how they use their powers.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe ways schools and communities work to establish responsibilities, fulfill roles of authority, and accomplish common tasks.
description_plain: Your child describes how schools and communities set responsibilities, roles, and work together to get things done.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe ways people impact communities which may include:
  • welcoming a new classmate/neighbor
  • developing classroom rules and procedures
  • contributing unused food to a food bank
  • picking up trash
description_plain: Your child talks about ways people help their communities, like welcoming new neighbors, making rules, donating food, or picking up trash.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the purpose of rules and laws.
description_plain: Your child explains why rules and laws are important.

subject: Social Studies
description: Follow agreed-upon rules for listening, consensus building, and voting procedures in the classroom.
description_plain: Your child follows rules for listening, making decisions together, and voting in the classroom.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify personal responsibilities of being a good citizen in the community.
description_plain: Your child can name ways to be a responsible and helpful member of their community.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe state and national symbols and patriotic songs:
  • American flag
  • Flag etiquette
  • Star Spangled Banner
  • Recitation of Pledge of Allegiance
  • "Arkansas" by Eva Ware Barnett
  • White-Tailed Deer
  • Milk
  • Square Dance
  • Diamond
description_plain: Your child will recognize important state and national symbols and songs like the American flag, the Pledge of Allegiance, and the Star Spangled Banner.

subject: Social Studies
description: Discuss the purpose of government.
description_plain: Your child understands that government exists to help organize and protect people in a community.

subject: Social Studies
description:
Describe the roles of people who hold positions of authority which may include:
  • Teachers lead a classroom and help students learn
  • School principal makes decisions for the school
  • Police officers protect and serve their communities
  • Fire/rescue workers help people in need
description_plain: Your child can explain what teachers, principals, police officers, and firefighters do to help others.

subject: Social Studies
description: Connect classroom rules and a student handbook as documents that establish the values of fairness and equality in a school community.
description_plain: Your child understands that classroom rules and the student handbook help make school fair and safe for everyone.

subject: Social Studies
description: Understand the impact of origins, structures, and functions of institutions and laws on society and citizens. This includes personal civic rights, roles, responsibilities, and processes by which laws are made and amended.
description_plain: Your child understands how laws and institutions affect people’s rights and responsibilities and how laws are made and changed.

description_plain: Your child understands important world history periods from 1450 to today, including how societies and cultures have changed and how people view history.

description_plain: Your child understands important events and changes in the U.S. from the Great Depression and World War II to today, including how people see and tell history.

description_plain: Your child understands key events and changes in the U.S. from the Civil War to the early 1900s, including how people view and explain history.

description_plain: Your child understands important U.S. history from the country’s beginnings through 1850, including how society, economy, and politics changed.
